data.title: 3 things to look out for on Monday
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages ended mixed on Friday, but the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) achieved another record close as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended +0.8% reaching the all-time high close of 7,165.08 points, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed +1.6%, also at the record close of 24,836.60 points. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.2%.

Now, here are three focus points for investors on Monday:

  • Notable companies due to report include Verizon (VZ), Public Storage (PSA), Nucor (NUE), and Domino's Pizza (DPZ). Options trading implies a double-digit swing for Rambus (RMBS) after it reports.
  • Pershing Square USA (PSUS) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. Buyers of the new company will get exposure to Bill Ackman’s investing vehicle plus a small stake in the Pershing Square (PS) management company. The analyst quiet period expires on HMH Holding (HMH) to free up analysts to post ratings.
  • Monday has U.S. Treasury auctions for 3-month (US3M) bills, 6-month (US6M) bills, 2-year (US2Y) notes and 5-year (US5Y) notes, ahead of the Federal Reserve's rate decision later in the week.

data.title: Sensient targets high-single to double-digit 2026 local-currency growth while lifting Color Group outlook to double-digit growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sensient Technologies (SXT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Paul Manning said the company had “a very strong start to 2026,” citing “7% local currency revenue growth, 10% local currency adjusted EBITDA growth, and 14% local currency adjusted EPS growth,” and added that results “exceeded our early expectations and position us nicely for the year.”

  • Manning highlighted Color Group momentum and conversions: “Commercial activity around natural color conversions continues to be very strong and the momentum is building,” adding, “we are starting to see an uptick in customer orders for conversion of their synthetically colored products in the U.S., and the pipeline to $1 billion continues to look very promising.”

  • Manning framed strategy and capital focus around the U.S. shift to natural colors: “the preparations for the wholesale conversion of synthetic colors to natural colors in the United States remains our priority and current strategic focus,” and he reiterated that “the U.S. conversion to natural colors is the single largest opportunity in Sensient’s history.”

  • On geopolitics, Manning said, “we do not have any significant operations in the Middle East,” while adding the company is “working to mitigate any potential supply chain risks that may result from the overall increase in fuel and certain commodity prices,” and that with the war in Iran, “we have proven our ability to adjust prices where necessary.”

  • Tobin Tornehl said, “Sensient’s revenue was $435.8 million in the first quarter of 2026,” and added, “cash flow used in operations was $14 million in the first quarter of 2026,” with “capital expenditures…$29 million,” while noting “our net debt to credit adjusted EBITDA is 2.4x as of March 31, 2026.”

Outlook

  • Manning said, “we are increasing our local currency ranges for the year,” and the company “now expect[s] our local currency revenue to be up high-single to double-digits,” with “local currency adjusted EBITDA and EPS to grow at high-single to double-digit rates.”

  • Tornehl reiterated the cadence expectation: “We continue to expect acceleration in revenue and EBITDA growth in the second half of the year.”

  • Tornehl added near-term line items: “We expect our second quarter interest expense to be approximately $9 million, and we expect our second quarter adjusted tax rate to be approximately 25%,” and “we still expect the impact of currency on EPS to be immaterial for the year.”

  • On capital allocation, Manning said the company still expects “capital expenditures of $150 million to $170 million in 2026,” and “we do not anticipate any share buybacks at this time.”

Financial Results

  • Tornehl reported profitability and below-the-line items: “Operating income was $66.7 million in the first quarter of 2026,” “interest expense was $7.9 million,” and “the Company’s consolidated adjusted tax rate was 24.9% in the first quarter of 2026.”

  • Tornehl said, “Foreign currency translation had approximately a $0.06 benefit on EPS in the first quarter of 2026,” and also stated that “cash flow used in operations was $14 million,” alongside “capital expenditures…$29 million.”

  • On leverage and funding for the buildout, Tornehl said the company expects “higher investments in inventory throughout the year to prepare for increased natural Color Conversion revenue,” and that leverage is “expected to increase further with our leverage ratio entering the upper twos later in the year.”

Q&A

  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked what drove the better-than-expected quarter (wins vs. faster conversions vs. inventory load-in). Chairman, CEO, President Paul Manning: “the simple answer is we got more wins than we thought,” including “more natural color conversions than I had anticipated,” plus “we didn’t see as much of that tariff distortions that I was concerned about on the last call.”

  • Panjabi, Baird: asked about growth cadence through the year. CEO Manning: “Q2 will look pretty similar to Q1,” and “Q4 is where you’ll see perhaps a more decided inflection point in natural colors.”

  • Panjabi, Baird: asked whether the TiO2 opportunity is part of the $1 billion goal. CEO Manning: “titanium dioxide, from a regulatory standpoint, is actually considered natural colors,” but “I hadn’t contemplated that in the $1 billion.”

  • Joshua Spector, UBS: pressed on customer timeline slippage risks and confidence vs. 3 months ago. CEO Manning: “I’m very exceedingly confident,” adding, “there is no slowdown at any of these customers,” and pointing to “January 1, 2027…[and] January 1, 2028” as key target dates.

  • Spector, UBS: asked about Color margins and OpEx investment levels. CEO Manning: “net-net for the year, we will be flattish on the EBITDA in the Color Group,” explaining the balance between depreciation timing, staffing, and R&D/application work.

  • Lawrence Solow, CJS: asked if better Color revenue timing explains improved margin expectations with no change in investments. CEO Manning: “revenue was a little bit better than we thought,” and “we did better than we thought…so therefore, the EBITDA was not down as I had thought it might be.”

  • Solow, CJS: asked about “musical chairs” supply-chain readiness ahead of 2027–2028. CEO Manning: “the bulk of the activity is going to be in 2027,” while warning of a possible “tipping point” and saying, “we’re hitting it hard…on capital…[and] the supply chain.”

  • Solow, CJS: asked about FDA involvement in natural colors approvals. CEO Manning: “Colors actually have to go through a…approval process with the FDA,” and “there’s a lot more…petitioning to use a new natural color in the market.”

  • Ming Tang, BNP Paribas: asked for an update on conversion revenue (previously about $5 million). CEO Manning: “when you take that back-half and you take Q1 of this year, now we’ve invoiced about $20 million or so towards that goal of natural colors.”

  • Tang, BNP Paribas: challenged the EPS guidance math vs. Q1 dynamics and risks. CEO Manning: “as you get below EBITDA…you then start to have to factor in things like interest and tax,” and CFO Tornehl added, “we expect our overall interest expense to be up about $6 million throughout this year,” while noting Q1 FX benefited EPS but “in the back half of the year, that would become more of a headwind.”

  • Tang, BNP Paribas: asked about raw-material inflation and disruption exposure. CEO Manning: “we believe that there is a sufficient amount of inflationary inputs that we’re going to need to take pricing to address that…low-single-digit magnitude,” and said pressures include “logistical inflation,” packaging, and “synthetic colors…derived” from petroleum-based inputs.

  • Spector, UBS (follow-up): asked if Q2 guidance bakes in logistics cost headwinds and price offsets. CEO Manning: “I’m not assuming any bad, and I’m not assuming any good,” and “I’m also not assuming any pricing in Q2.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative/pressing around timing, margins, and guidance math, including Spector’s “a lot of investors are concerned that things could slip” and Tang’s question about an “implied downgrade on the rest of the year.”

  • Management tone was positive/confident in prepared remarks and firm in Q&A, with Manning saying, “I’m very exceedingly confident,” and “there is no slowdown at any of these customers,” while acknowledging variability in “the precise distribution” of monthly conversions.

  • Versus last quarter, management language shifted from expecting investment-driven margin pressure to indicating Color EBITDA margin should be “flattish” for the year, while analyst questioning remained focused on the conversion timeline and profit leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised: in Q1 2026, Manning said full-year local-currency revenue is now expected “up high-single to double-digits,” versus Q4 2025 guidance of “mid-single digit to double digits,” and Q1 2026 raised expected local-currency adjusted EPS growth to “high-single to double-digit” from the prior “mid-single to high-single-digit.”

  • Color Group outlook tightened upward: in Q1 2026, Manning said, “I now expect the Color Group to deliver double-digit local currency revenue growth in 2026,” versus Q4 2025’s “high single-digit to double-digit range.”

  • On tariffs and Asia Pacific, Q1 2026 commentary indicated less disruption than feared (“we didn’t see as much of that tariff distortions”), compared with Q4 2025 where tariff activity and supply-chain disruptions were described as weighing on Asia Pacific results and early-2026 expectations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged potential cost and supply-chain impacts tied to geopolitics: Manning referenced “trade, tariffs, and geopolitics,” and said the company is mitigating possible impacts from “overall increase in fuel and certain commodity prices.”

  • Inflation and logistics were identified as likely requiring pricing actions: Manning said inflationary inputs may require “a modest amount of price increase,” spanning logistics and certain synthetic and natural inputs.

  • Financing and FX were cited as swing factors for EPS: Tornehl said interest expense is expected to rise with investment and inventory builds, and management said FX could be “more of a headwind” in the back half even if “immaterial for the year” overall.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as an early upside print driven by “more wins than we thought,” and paired it with higher full-year growth ranges and a more explicit expectation for “double-digit” Color Group growth in 2026, while emphasizing that increased investment and a rising interest burden remain part of the plan to prepare capacity, supply chain, and technical resources for the U.S. natural color conversion opportunity tied to customers’ 2027–2028 timelines and Sensient’s $1 billion natural color sales goal.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Heidmar Maritime Holdings faces Nasdaq $1 compliance rule breach
data.description:
• Heidmar Maritime (HMR) announced on Friday that it received a notice from Nasdaq saying its stock price has stayed below the required $1 minimum for 30 straight trading days, so it is currently not compliant with listing rules.
  • The company now has a 180-day grace period (until Oct. 19, 2026) to resolve this issue and get back in compliance.
  • To regain compliance, its stock needs to close at $1 or above for at least 10 consecutive trading days.
  • Until then, its business operations remain unaffected, and the stock continues trading on Nasdaq.
  • The stock is trading higher at ~$0.82.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Phillips Edison raises 2026 outlook, targets 5.9% Nareit FFO per share growth and reaffirms $400M-$500M acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Phillips Edison & Company (PECO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We're pleased to report another quarter of strong results" and "we're pleased to increase our full year 2026 guidance," with "Nareit FFO per share growth of 4.7%, core FFO per share growth of 6.2% and same-center NOI growth of 3.5%." (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Jeffrey Edison)

  • "One of the dynamics we're watching closely is the gap between private and public market pricing of assets" and "it's why the PECO team stays disciplined about accessing the most efficient capital," including "institutional joint ventures" and "asset recycling." (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Edison)

  • "We continue to see high retailer demand with no current signs of slowing" and said "lease portfolio occupancy remained high at 97.1%," with "leased anchor occupancy" at "98.4%" and "leased inline occupancy" at "95%." (President Robert Myers)

  • "PECO delivered comparable renewal rent spreads of 21.2%" and "comparable new rent spreads" of "36.2%," while "bad debt was lower than expected in the first quarter at around 60 basis points of revenue." (President Myers)

  • "PECO has 19 projects under active construction" with "total investment" of "approximately $74 million" and "average estimated yields between 9% and 12%," and said "year-to-date acquisition activity through this week reflects $185 million" with "approximately $150 million" expected to close "by the end of the second quarter." (President Myers)

  • "First quarter 2026 Nareit FFO increased to $92.9 million or $0.67 per diluted share" and "first quarter core FFO increased to $96.4 million or $0.69 per diluted share." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer John Caulfield)

Outlook

  • "We are pleased to increase our 2026 guidance" and said key drivers included "continued strong operating environment, strong year-to-date acquisitions activity and our recent bond offering." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield)

  • "Our updated guidance for 2026 Nareit FFO per share reflects a 5.9% increase over 2025 at the midpoint" and "our updated guidance for 2026 core FFO per share represents a 5.8% increase over 2025 at the midpoint." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield)

  • "We are reiterating our full year 2026 guidance of 3% to 4% same-center NOI growth" and "reaffirm our full year 2026 guidance of $400 million to $500 million in gross acquisitions at PECO share," alongside planned dispositions of "between $100 million and $200 million" in 2026. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield)

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management moved from describing 2026 growth as "mid-single digits" to citing midpoint growth of "5.9%" (Nareit FFO per share) and "5.8%" (core FFO per share). (Chairman & CEO Jeffrey Edison; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield)

Financial Results

  • "First quarter 2026 Nareit FFO increased to $92.9 million" and "same-center NOI increased 3.5% in the quarter, primarily due to higher revenue, which was driven by increases in average rents and economic occupancy." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield)

  • On funding and rate protection, "we completed a public debt offering of $350 million" of "4.75% senior notes due 2033," and at quarter-end reported "$810 million in liquidity," "net debt to trailing 12-month annualized adjusted EBITDAre" of "5.3x," a weighted average interest rate of "4.4%," weighted average maturity of "5.8 years," and "94%" fixed-rate debt. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield)

  • On acquisitions and pricing, management said year-to-date acquisitions included "10 acquisitions" and "we're buying these at a cap rate of about 6.6%, 6.7%" while "still solving for our unlevered returns above 9%." (President Myers)

Q&A

  • Andrew Reale, BofA Securities: "any incremental changes" from discretionary/off-price tenants? President Myers: "we have the best renewal pipeline and new leasing pipeline that we've seen at about the last 6 to 9 months" and "we're not seeing any pullbacks"; he added, "Occupancy costs continue to remain very strong at 10%."

  • Haendel St. Juste, Mizuho: any buyer pullback and potential to slow deployment? Chairman & CEO Edison: "there is a very ample supply of product coming on the market" and "there are more buyers"; President Myers: "We reviewed 195 deals this year compared to 115 last year" and cap rates are "consistent with that 6.5% to 6.75%."

  • Michael Griffin, Evercore ISI: can grocer lease negotiations add bumps/shorten options? President Myers: grocers often have options "for the next 30 years" that are "typically flat," but PECO seeks bumps when renegotiating and also finds value via "consents on restrictions" and "no build areas."

  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: views on development/redevelopment? Chairman & CEO Edison: "we have about $70 million of development work"; President Myers cited land near grocers, including "about 5.8 acres" in "North Pointe, Florida," and said returns are "between 9% and 12%."

  • Ronald Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: path to "96%, 97%" inline occupancy and local-tenant mix? President Myers: local share "25% or 26%... right on top of each other" and cited a "bounty targeted space approach" with "28 deals" executed and "another 24" in progress.

  • Cooper Clark, Wells Fargo: retention down—intentional recapture? President Myers: "Our retention rate this quarter was 88%" due to "a 64,000 square foot box" planned to vacate; excluding it, retention would have been "92.4%."

  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: why raise FFO guidance without component changes? Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield: drivers included "bad debt... near the lower end of our range" and "the bond offering was at an interest rate lower than we budgeted," while noting "the SOFR curve... is higher than where we started the year."

  • Todd Thomas, KeyBanc: quantify public/private valuation spread and actions? Chairman & CEO Edison: "there is 50 to 75 basis points difference" and said the response is to look at "joint ventures," "issuing equity," and "selling assets"; on dispositions, "there is a little bit more lean in."

  • Floris Van Dijkum, Ladenburg: rationale for unanchored centers? Chairman & CEO Edison: everyday retail is "a much more inefficient market" and aims for "outsized returns"; President Myers said the 12-asset subset totals "about $221 million," with purchases around a "6.9% cap" and targeting "between 10% and 11% unlevered returns."

  • Juan Sanabria, BMO: where is new supply emerging? Chairman & CEO Edison: supply is "a really small amount" and "we're just not seeing much at all in our markets."

  • Sydnie Rohme, Barclays: why keep $5M-$8M collectibility adjustment range? Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield: tenant stress signals were limited as "the absolute count of neighbors" in focus "declined this quarter" and "overall, things are quite good."

  • Michael Mueller, JPMorgan: JV investment sensitivity to equity cost? Chairman & CEO Edison: JV strategy is "primarily to expand what we can buy" and PECO is "buying things in our JVs that we would not buy on the balance sheet."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to neutral, probing macro sensitivity, transaction competition, and guidance mechanics, including "any incremental changes in their tone" (Reale) and "what drove the higher earnings expectation?" (Goldsmith).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly pointing to stability and leasing demand, including "we're not seeing any pullbacks" (President Myers) and "it's early" but "we're more optimistic about the year" (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield).

  • Versus Q4 2025, management carried forward similar confidence about resilience, but added more emphasis on capital-market dislocation, including "50 to 75 basis points difference" between public and private markets. (Chairman & CEO Edison)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1 2026, PECO said it "increase[d] our full year 2026 guidance" and specified midpoint growth rates of "5.9%" (Nareit FFO per share) and "5.8%" (core FFO per share), compared with Q4 2025 describing 2026 growth as "mid-single digits." (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield; Chairman & CEO Edison)

  • Capital structure messaging shifted from planning to raise fixed-rate mix (Q4: target "approximately 90%" and finished at "85%") to reporting execution (Q1: "94%" fixed-rate debt following a "$350 million" bond). (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield)

  • Leasing remained a centerpiece in both quarters, with Q1 detailing occupancy at "97.1%" and renewal/new spreads of "21.2%" and "36.2%," while Q4 emphasized year-end occupancy of "97.3%" and Q4 renewal/new spreads of "20%" and "34.3%." (President Myers)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged macro uncertainty and rate volatility, stating "Interest rates have been volatile" and "the global trade picture is shifting." (Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO Edison)

  • The CFO highlighted rate sensitivity, saying "one thing we're watching is the SOFR curve," and kept collectibility guidance intact despite Q1 outperformance. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Caulfield)

  • Analysts pressed on potential tenant stress and underwriting competition, while management responded that it is "not seeing any cracks" and cited continued supply of deals and leasing strength. (President Myers)

Final Takeaway

PECO management emphasized a stable operating backdrop for grocery-anchored and necessity-based retail while raising 2026 FFO per-share growth expectations, reiterating 3% to 4% same-center NOI growth and $400 million to $500 million of acquisitions. Executives tied the higher outlook to leasing strength, active acquisitions, and a new $350 million unsecured bond that increased fixed-rate debt, while also highlighting a widening public-versus-private valuation gap as a key factor shaping dispositions and joint-venture capital decisions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Scorpio Tankers leads seven ocean transportation stocks with new Buy ratings at Jefferies
data.description:
Jefferies on Friday initiated bullish coverage Friday of seven marine transportation stocks that are currently enjoying a boost from rising energy prices due to the war in Iran, including Buy ratings on International Seaways (INSW), Scorpio Tankers (STNG), Navios Maritime Partners (NMM), and Star Bulk Carriers (SBLK), with respective price targets of $90, $90, $85, and $29.

Sentiment for tankers remains strong due to a favorable supply and demand picture, boosted bygeopolitics, and "so long as ships soak up capacity from vessel sanctions, long-haul voyages from Venezuela, Russia/Ukraine, OPEC+ barrels, and supply remains tight from the Iranian conflict, the sector outlook remains appealing," Jefferies analyst Stephanie Moore wrote.

Supported by strong tanker fundamentals and geopolitics, Moore said she favors International Seaways (INSW) for its balanced exposure to crude and product that positions it well for periods of heightened volatility, and Scorpio Tankers (STNG) for its fully scrubber-fitted fleet, a net cash balance sheet, and a higher dividend.

The analyst said she likes Navios Maritime (NMM) for its diversified platform and recent dividend increase, and Star Bulk Carriers (SBLK) for its debt reduction, buybacks, and dividends amid a constructive dry bulk outlook.

The analyst started Dorian LPG (LPG) with a Buy recommendation and $42, believing rates for liquefied petroleum gas likely will stay elevated due to "favorable LPG demand dynamics" as long as the war continues.

Global Ship Lease (GSL) was initiated at Buy with a $45 PT, with Moore citing the company's significant revenue backlog that cushions it against consumer uncertainty, its disciplined capital allocation, and strong balance sheet, while cautioning on the overall container sector due to the pressure on consumers from higher energy prices and an "onslaught" of new ship deliveries.

The analyst rated Capital Clean Energy Carriers (CCEC) at Hold with a $20 PT, saying the LNG market is "off to a weak start" this year.

data.title: Westamerica Bancorporation extends stock repurchase authorization
data.description:
• Westamerica Bancorporation (WABC) said on Friday its board had approved a 2M share increase to its stock repurchase authorization, leaving 2.79M shares available under the expanded plan, or about 11.8% of shares outstanding as of March 31, 2026.
  • The plan had permitted share buybacks via open-market or private deals through Dec. 31, 2026.

data.title: Vesta signals development restart with 1.6 million sq ft pipeline and a $74.8M 2026 dividend
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Corporación Inmobiliaria Vesta (VTMX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Lorenzo Dominique Berho Carranza said Q1 delivered “a strong start to the year with solid leasing momentum and stable portfolio performance despite ongoing global tensions,” adding that Vesta is seeing “growing conviction from our tenants” reflected in new leases and expansions.
  • Berho reaffirmed execution priorities under Route 2030, saying the strategy is “centered on expanding a well-curated high-quality portfolio for disciplined development, leveraging our privileged land bank to capture demand,” and arguing “value creation in our space is driven more by quality than size.”
  • Berho highlighted Q1 leasing and occupancy, including “total first quarter leasing reaching approximately 1.6 million square feet” and “total portfolio occupancy reached 89.7% ... while stabilized and same-store occupancy reached 93.4% and 95%, respectively,” alongside demand strength in “electronics and aerospace” and “AI-related data center infrastructure.”
  • Berho said Vesta “selectively resumed development,” stating: “We launched 2 new projects in Mexico City and 1 in Tijuana during the first quarter, which brings our total development pipeline to approximately 1.6 million square feet.”
  • CFO Juan Felipe Sottil Achuttegui said, “On the top line, we delivered a solid start of the year, with total revenues increasing 14.4% to $76.7 million,” and linked drivers to “rental income from new leases and inflationary adjustments across our portfolios.”

Outlook

  • The company did not provide updated EPS or revenue guidance in this Q1 transcript; management reiterated confidence qualitatively, with Berho stating, “In closing, we are highly confident in our outlook,” and pointing to expectations that “a more favorable interest rate environment together with greater clarity around USMCA” could “support activity in the quarters ahead.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter call, the key shift in messaging was from setting explicit full-year 2026 targets (given in Q4) to emphasizing Q1 leasing traction, selective development restarts, and market-by-market commentary without reiterating full-year margin or revenue ranges.

Financial Results

  • Vesta reported “total rental income increased to $76.7 million,” and CFO Sottil said “rental revenues reached $74 million, a 14.1% sequential increase.”
  • Profitability metrics were disclosed as: “Adjusted net operating income increased 13.4% to $70.47 million,” with “adjusted NOI margin ... 95.1%,” and “Adjusted EBITDA totaled $62.1 million ... while margin contracted ... to 83.9%.”
  • Cash flow and leverage items included “$206 million in cash and cash equivalents,” “total debt of $1.2 billion,” “Net-debt to EBITDA stood at 4.1x,” and “our loan-to-value ratio was 26% ... reflecting the prepayment of the remaining $118 million MetLife III facilities.”
  • Capital return was updated after the quarter: “on April 22, 2026, Vesta's shareholders approved a $74.8 million dividend for 2026 representing a 7.5% increase year-over-year.”

Q&A

  • Piero Trotta, Citigroup Inc., Research Division asked why Vesta started spec development in Tijuana despite cited market vacancy and asked about leasing spread sustainability; Chief Executive Officer Berho answered that the Tijuana start is “a continuation of our existing project mega region,” driven by a “good pipeline,” plus location, labor, logistics, and “good access to energy,” and added on spreads: “I think that the spreads will continue to be in a 10% to 13% range somehow.”
  • Gordon Lee, Banco BTG Pactual S.A., Research Division asked about consolidation and whether it improves discipline; CEO Berho said consolidation is tied to “a lot of capital chasing for transaction,” helps “set pricing to transactions,” and added, “most of these consolidators do not have development capabilities.”
  • David Soto Soto, Scotiabank Global Banking and Markets, Research Division asked about “micro grid” regions and challenges; CEO Berho described current and planned spec activity across Mexico City, Tijuana, Guadalajara, and land in Monterrey (La Palma and Juarez), tying starts to permitting progress and demand from “electronics,” “AI,” “data center infrastructure,” “e-commerce,” “logistics,” and “medical devices.”
  • Ernst Mortenkotter, GBM Grupo Bursátil Mexicano, asked what signals drove reactivating development and how funding capacity is viewed; CEO Berho cited internal demand indicators and highlighted Guadalajara pre-leasing success, while CFO Sottil said, “we have a very strong balance sheet,” and “we will tap the market whenever possible,” including “sell properties” or “do equity.”
  • Adrian Huerta, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division asked about asset recycling and yield-on-cost; CEO Berho said yield on cost is “in the 10% range,” citing Mexico City “9.8%” and other markets “10.5%, 11%,” while CFO Sottil said, “we will always be open” to recycling but “we sell selectively.”
  • Rodolfo Ramos, Banco Bradesco BBI S.A., Research Division asked whether consolidation impacts northern markets spreads or commercial efforts; CEO Berho said Mexico industrial real estate is “very fragmented” and most acquisitions are in “secondary and tertiary markets.”
  • Carlos Peyrelongue, BofA Securities, Research Division asked whether occupancy can increase and which markets drive it; CFO Sottil said, “we generally don't project occupancy forward-looking,” while CEO Berho pointed to “Monterrey ... Apodaca project” marketing momentum and “Bajio ... Queretaro.”
  • Igor Machado, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division asked about construction costs amid Middle East conflict and San Luis Potosí demand; CEO Berho said the company has “not seen any material adjustments” to construction cost, noted FX monitoring, and said projects under construction are often “on guaranteed maximum price,” while calling San Luis Potosí “a slower market” tied to auto demand but “should not have a major impact.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, pressing on Tijuana vacancy, cost inflation risk, funding needs, and occupancy durability, including Trotta’s reference to “around 16% vacancy” in Tijuana.
  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and confident in Q&A, with Berho stating “we are highly confident in our outlook,” and on spreads saying, “we are confident that, that will continue.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management maintained confidence but shifted from broad 2026–2027 cycle framing to more specific defense of selective spec development (notably Tijuana) and a heavier emphasis on capital allocation flexibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current call emphasized restarting development starts (Mexico City and Tijuana) and detailed drivers like energy access and submarket differentiation; the prior call emphasized positioning for a “powerful demand cycle beginning in 2026 and accelerating into 2027” and laid out full-year 2026 guidance.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from USMCA resilience and guidance assumptions (Q4) toward market-level vacancy (Tijuana), construction-cost sensitivity, and near-term funding options (Q1).
  • Management maintained a similar “quality not scale” posture, but Q1 included more granular discussion of transaction-market consolidation as a valuation reference point and explicit yield-on-cost ranges tied to land cost.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited “ongoing global tensions” and said it is monitoring whether the Middle East conflict has “implications ... on the construction cost,” while adding it has “not seen any material adjustments” and uses “guaranteed maximum price” structures to reduce cost volatility.
  • Market risk was discussed as “rising vacancy in certain regions,” with Berho characterizing it as “a correction, not a structural slowdown,” and highlighting that weaker performance in places like Tijuana can be linked to “less experienced developers” and constraints such as energy and logistics.
  • Funding and capital markets flexibility remained a recurring Q&A topic, with CFO Sottil saying the company can use cash, debt, asset sales, or equity and will “always be flexible.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as a demand-confirmation quarter, pointing to approximately 1.6 million square feet of leasing, stable occupancy, and improving visibility that supported restarting selective development, including launches in Mexico City and Tijuana that brought the pipeline to approximately 1.6 million square feet. Executives highlighted balance sheet actions (including the MetLife III prepayment and fully unsecured debt profile) and reiterated a disciplined approach to development and asset recycling, while shareholders approved a $74.8 million 2026 dividend that management said represents a 7.5% year-over-year increase.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq snatch another record as U.S. continues ceasefire negotiations with Iran
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages ended mixed on Friday, but the S&P 500 ((SP500)) and the Nasdaq ((COMP:IND)) achieved another record close as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended +0.8% reaching the all-time high close of 7,165.08 points, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed +1.6%, also at the record close of 24,836.60 points.

Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.2%.

Intel (INTC) shares jumped 23.3% after its Q1 results delivered a beat, and now its second-quarter revenue outlook is expected to surpass what analysts anticipated. Other semiconductors also rose, including Nvidia (NVDA) +4.3%, TSMC (TSM) +5.1%, AMD (AMD) +13.9%, Arm Holdings (ARM) +14.7%, and Qualcomm (QCOM) +11.2%.

Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points lower at 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting ambassadors of both countries in Washington.

"The meeting went very well! The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah. The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by THREE WEEKS," he said via Truth Social.

In addition, Trump is sending envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to Islamabad this weekend for talks with Iran's foreign minister, according to reports.

On Thursday, Trump also ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Moreover, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy laid more mines in the Strait of Hormuz this week, according to a report by Axios.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly lower at $94, while Brent (CO1:COM) was last around $106 per barrel.

On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April—to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March—according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

data.title: Walmart dips after a six-session rally
data.description:
Shares of Walmart (WMT) fell 1.60% to close at $129.92 on Friday, ending a six-session winning streak.

The stock has gained around 5.8% between April 16 and April 23, compared to a 0.95% rise in the S&P 500 during the same period.

Shares of the retail giant edged lower, as sentiment turned cautious after Democratic lawmakers urged major retailers to pass on any potential tariff refunds to consumers, raising uncertainty around pricing flexibility and the potential impact on margins, while also discouraging the use of such funds for buybacks or executive compensation.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, WMT is rated a Hold, with a score of 3.38 out of 5, receiving an A+ for profitability, but an F in terms of valuation.

However, a recent Seeking Alpha analysis rated WMT a Sell, citing an unjustified surge in valuation despite slowing growth trends, and the analyst pointed out that “the stock’s valuation has gone up, in my view, in an unreasonable way.”

While on Wall Street, 38 out of 42 analysts rate the stock a buy or higher, three recommend a hold, while one of them suggests a sell or lower.

Despite the modest decline, shares remain 17.8% up year-to-date and have gained around 5.8% in the past month.

data.title: iHeartMedia gains on report of merger talks with Sirius XM
data.description:
iHeartMedia (IHRT) jumped 14% after a report that it's holding talks with Sirius XM (SIRI) about a possible combination. Shares of Sirius XM fell 5%.

The talks are preliminary, and there's no certainty that a transaction will take place, according to a  Bloomberg report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

A deal would combine the largest radio station owner in in the US with the largest satellite radio service. iHeartMedia (IHRT) has a market cap of $573 million, while Sirius XM (SIRI) is valued at $9.4 billion.

 iHeartMedia and Sirius XM declined to comment to Bloomberg.

iHeartMedia is set to report Q1 results on May 11, while Sirius XM is scheduled for next Thursday.
